name: Tlalocan
description: Tlalocan, in Aztec mythology, was the paradise ruled by the rain god Tlaloc and his consort Chalchiuhtlicue. It was a realm of eternal spring, a lush and verdant land filled with an abundance of flowers, fruits, and clear-flowing streams. Unlike other afterlives in Aztec belief, Tlalocan was not earned through heroic deeds in battle but was reserved for those who died by drowning, lightning strike, or diseases associated with water, such as dropsy. Those who entered Tlalocan enjoyed a life of ease and plenty, free from hardship and want. It was a place of perpetual happiness and abundance, a stark contrast to the often harsh realities of earthly life. The entrance to Tlalocan was believed to be located in springs, caves, and mountaintops, places where water naturally emerged from the earth. Shamans and priests often sought to communicate with Tlaloc and the spirits of Tlalocan to ensure favorable weather and abundant harvests for the Aztec people. The imagery of Tlalocan is deeply connected to fertility, agricultural prosperity, and the life-giving power of water. It represents a utopian vision of a world untouched by drought and famine, a testament to the importance of water in Aztec culture and belief.
subtitle: Paradise of Tlaloc, land of plenty

keyMyths.title: The Journey to Tlalocan
keyMyths.description: Those who died from drowning or water-related causes were believed to travel to Tlalocan, guided by the Tlalocas. There, they would live in eternal bliss, surrounded by abundance and free from suffering.
keyMyths.source: Various Aztec codices and historical accounts

keyMyths.title: Tlaloc's Gifts
keyMyths.description: Tlaloc, from Tlalocan, bestows rain and fertility upon the earth. The priests performed rituals and sacrifices to appease him, ensuring a bountiful harvest and preventing droughts.
keyMyths.source: Bernardino de Sahagún, *General History of the Things of New Spain*

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún
sources.work: General History of the Things of New Spain
sources.description: Comprehensive ethnographic account of Aztec culture and religion.
sources.text: Comprehensive ethnographic account of Aztec culture and religion.

sources.author: David Carrasco
